coredata.dc:title: Copper-chelation-induced ratiometric fluorescence response for sensitive ratiometric detection of zoledronic acid: implications for monitoring in renal impairment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
Mostafa, Aya M.
Barker, James
Bin Jardan, Yousef A.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Zoledronic acid (ZOL) is a potent bisphosphonate widely used in the treatment of bone disorders, including osteoporosis, Paget’s disease, and bone metastases. Despite its clinical importance, current methods for ZOL detection in biological samples often require complex instrumentation and lengthy procedures. This study presents the first ratiometric fluorometric sensor for ZOL detection in real biological samples utilizing a dual emission carbon dots (D@CDs)/Cu2+ system. The sensing mechanism relies on ZOL’s strong affinity for copper ions. When ZOL is introduced into the D@CDs/Cu2+ system, it forms stable complexes with Cu2+, causing a measurable decrease in fluorescence emission at 370 nm while simultaneously enhancing emission at 578 nm. This dual-wavelength response enables highly sensitive ratiometric detection of ZOL. Comprehensive characterization of the D@CDs and the detection mechanism was conducted using various spectroscopic and analytical techniques. The developed method demonstrates excellent analytical performance with a wide linear detection range (10.0–90.0 nM) and strong correlation (R2 = 0.9949). The system achieves a remarkably low limit of detection (2.15 nM), making it suitable for measuring ZOL concentrations in biological fluids at clinically relevant levels. The practical utility of this method was validated through successful application to rat serum samples. Furthermore, the technique was employed to monitor ZOL levels in both healthy rats and those with experimentally induced renal dysfunction, enabling comparative pharmacokinetic analysis between these groups. The method’s sensitivity and reliability make it a valuable tool for pharmacokinetic studies and therapeutic drug monitoring of ZOL in clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zoledronic acid
Ratiometric detection
Copper ions
Pharmacokinetic
Renal dysfunction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantum yield of the prepared D@CDs
Induction of chronic renal impairment in experimental animals
Structural and morphological studies of D@CDs
Sensing of ZOL in spiked rat serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and size distribution histogram, (B) XRD pattern, (C) FTIR spectrum and (D) XPS spectrum of D@CDs.

(A) UV–vis, photoluminescence, fluorescent emission spectra of D@CDs with the characteristic peaks at 370 nm and 578 nm (blue line) after excitation at 270 nm (red line). (B) Emission profiles of D@CDs were analyzed across excitation wavelengths spanning 230 to 290 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of D@CDs/Cu2+ in the ZOL concentration range 0 – 90.0 nM. (B) The linear relationship between the (F578/F370) and the ZOL concentration (10.0–90.0 nM). (C) Fluorescence diagram of the ratiometric fluorescent probe with different concentrations of ZOL (10.0 nM–90.0 nM) under UV lamp irradiation. (D) Chromaticity diagram. The coordinate points correspond to the fluorescent colors in (C).

(A) Fluorescence spectral overlay of D@CDs, D@CDs/Cu2+, D@CDs/Cu2+/ZOL, (B) Ultraviolet visible spectrum of ZOL, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of D@CDs and (C) Ultraviolet visible spectral overlay of D@CDs, D@CDs/Cu2+, D@CDs/Cu2+/ZOL. (D) Zeta potential of D@CDs, D@CDs/Cu2+, D@CDs/Cu2+/ZOL.
Pharmacokinetic characterization of ZOL in rats with and without renal impairment.
Comparison of the reported fluorometric methods for ZOL detection with proposed method.

row.entry: Real serum samples and pharmacokinetic applications

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of ZOL in rat serum samples via the proposed and the reported methods (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Copper-chelation-induced ratiometric fluorescence response for sensitive ratiometric detection of zoledronic acid: implications for monitoring in renal impairment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zoledronic acid (ZOL) is a potent bisphosphonate widely used in the treatment of bone disorders, including osteoporosis, Paget’s disease, and bone metastases. Despite its clinical importance, current methods for ZOL detection in biological samples often require complex instrumentation and lengthy procedures. This study presents the first ratiometric fluorometric sensor for ZOL detection in real biological samples utilizing a dual emission carbon dots (D@CDs)/Cu2+ system. The sensing mechanism relies on ZOL’s strong affinity for copper ions. When ZOL is introduced into the D@CDs/Cu2+ system, it forms stable complexes with Cu2+, causing a measurable decrease in fluorescence emission at 370 nm while simultaneously enhancing emission at 578 nm. This dual-wavelength response enables highly sensitive ratiometric detection of ZOL. Comprehensive characterization of the D@CDs and the detection mechanism was conducted using various spectroscopic and analytical techniques. The developed method demonstrates excellent analytical performance with a wide linear detection range (10.0–90.0 nM) and strong correlation (R2 = 0.9949). The system achieves a remarkably low limit of detection (2.15 nM), making it suitable for measuring ZOL concentrations in biological fluids at clinically relevant levels. The practical utility of this method was validated through successful application to rat serum samples. Furthermore, the technique was employed to monitor ZOL levels in both healthy rats and those with experimentally induced renal dysfunction, enabling comparative pharmacokinetic analysis between these groups. The method’s sensitivity and reliability make it a valuable tool for pharmacokinetic studies and therapeutic drug monitoring of ZOL in clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Zoledronic acid (ZOL) is a potent nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that plays a fundamental role in the management of bone-related disorders through its mechanism of inhibiting osteoclast-mediated bone resorption [1]. This medication serves as a cornerstone in treating conditions such as osteoporosis, Paget's disease, and bone metastases associated with cancers like multiple myeloma and solid tumors, with its effectiveness stemming from its high affinity for hydroxyapatite in bone tissue that allows selective targeting of active bone remodeling areas [2]. ZOL plays a crucial role in various medical treatments, offering benefits across several conditions. It significantly reduces fracture risk in both postmenopausal women and men suffering from osteoporosis. In cancer care, ZOL helps prevent bone complications in patients with bone metastases or multiple myeloma. The drug effectively normalizes bone metabolism in individuals with Paget's disease and aids in controlling elevated calcium levels associated with malignancies. A notable advantage of ZOL is its convenient yearly intravenous dosing schedule, which helps ensure that patients follow their prescribed treatment regimen more consistently [3,4]. ZOL is favored in clinical practice over other drugs with comparable therapeutic effects, such as clodronate, pamidronate, alendronate, and etidronate, due to its significantly higher efficacy. At equivalent doses, ZOL demonstrates effectiveness that is hundreds of times greater than these alternatives [5]. Despite its therapeutic benefits, the drug presents several risks and side effects, including acute phase reactions manifesting as flu-like symptoms after initial infusion, potential renal toxicity, rare but serious osteonecrosis of the jaw (particularly in cancer patients receiving high doses), hypocalcemia requiring supplementation, potential atypical femoral fractures with long-term use, and rare cases of cardiac arrhythmias [6,7]. After intravenous administration, ZOL exhibits a rapid increase in plasma concentration, followed by a swift decline due to its distribution to bone tissue and other tissues. After a single 5 mg IV infusion of ZOL for osteoporosis patients over 15 min, the peak plasma concentration (Cmax) typically ranges between 100 and 500 ng/mL [8]. In cancer patients receiving 4 mg IV every 3–4 weeks, peak levels are higher, ranging from 200 to 1000 ng/mL. Co-administration with cisplatin or the presence of renal impairment can significantly alter these levels, as ZOL is primarily excreted by the kidneys [9]. Renal dysfunction can lead to reduced clearance, resulting in prolonged drug retention and higher plasma concentrations, which increases the risk of nephrotoxicity and hypocalcemia, necessitating careful dose adjustments and monitoring [10]. The determination of ZOL levels holds critical importance for therapeutic drug monitoring, renal function assessment, verification of patient compliance, support of research and development initiatives, ensuring regulatory compliance, and environmental monitoring of healthcare facility wastewater, though this analysis faces significant challenges due to the compound's lack of chromophore for UV detection, high polarity affecting chromatographic separation, and typically low concentrations in biological matrices, necessitating advanced analytical techniques.

Previous methods for the determination of ZOL have primarily relied on techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled with UV detection [11], or mass spectrometry (MS) [12]. However, these methods face significant limitations due to the unique physicochemical properties of ZOL, including its lack of a chromophore, high polarity, and low molecular weight, which complicate separation and detection. UV detection often requires derivatization to enhance sensitivity, adding complexity and time to the analysis [13]. Although LC-MS offers higher sensitivity and specificity, it requires expensive instrumentation and expertise, limiting its accessibility. Additionally, the low concentrations of ZOL in biological matrices and its strong binding to bone tissue further challenge accurate quantification, underscoring the need for more robust, cost-effective, and sensitive analytical methods.

Carbon dots have emerged as remarkable fluorescent nanomaterials that are revolutionizing sensing applications due to their unique photophysical properties and versatile surface chemistry [14]. These quantum-sized carbon nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in diameter, exhibit intense fluorescence emission that can be precisely tuned across the visible spectrum by controlling their size, surface functionalization, and synthetic conditions. The inherent advantages of carbon dots, including their exceptional photostability, resistance to photobleaching, high quantum yields, and low toxicity, make them ideal candidates for developing sensitive fluorescence-based detection methods. Their surface can be readily modified with various functional groups, enabling specific molecular recognition of target analytes through mechanisms such as electron transfer, energy transfer, or inner filter effects, which manifest as changes in fluorescence intensity, lifetime, or spectral shifts [15]. The remarkable sensitivity of carbon dots' fluorescence to their local chemical environment allows for the detection of diverse analytes ranging from metal ions and small molecules to proteins and nucleic acids, often achieving detection limits in the nanomolar or even picomolar range [16,17]. Carbon dots are cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and compatible with biological systems, making them ideal for bioanalytical applications. Their functionality in water-based solutions and advantages over conventional fluorescent compounds and quantum dots enhance their utility in practical sensing technologies [18]. This work introduces a novel fluorometric method for ZOL determination, representing a significant advancement in analytical chemistry and pharmaceutical analysis. Our innovative approach harnesses the unique dual-emission properties of carbon dots coupled with copper(II) ions to create a sensitive and selective ratiometric detection system. The method's novelty lies in its exploitation of the distinctive interaction between ZOL and copper ions, which triggers a measurable reversal in the dual-emission behavior of the carbon dots – specifically, the initial copper-induced increase at 370 nm and decrease at 578 nm is reversed upon ZOL complexation. This ratiometric approach offers inherent advantages over single-emission methods by providing built-in correction for environmental interferences and instrumental fluctuations. The effective implementation of this approach in analyzing biological fluids highlights its real-world applicability in clinical environments, where precise measurement of ZOL concentrations is essential for therapeutic drug monitoring. Our study not only establishes a selective fluorometric method for the sensing of ZOL in real samples but also demonstrates its capability to accurately quantify ZOL levels in both healthy rats and those with impaired renal function, ensuring its applicability in pharmacokinetic and biomedical analyses.

Zoledronic acid (99.0 %) was purchased from Hikma Pharmaceuticals, Cairo, Egypt. Boneimc® vials containing 4.0 mg ZOL per 5 mL were purchased from a local drug store. Histidine, cysteine, citric acid, lactic acid, uric acid, vitamin D3, teriparatide, paracetamol, ibuprofen, aspirin, creatinine, BSA, glucose, cisplatin, and paclitaxel were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Sodium phosphate (Na3PO4), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium nitrate (KNO3), iron(III) chloride (FeCl3), zinc sulfate (ZnSO4), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), iron(II) sulfate (FeSO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), and potassium nitrate (KNO3) were purchased from Merck, Darmstadt, Germany. Ultra-pure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.cm was utilized throughout all experiments.
All details about the instruments utilized are provided in the Supplementary Data file.

The synthesis of dual-emission carbon dots (D@CDs) was carried out through an optimized hydrothermal method. The synthetic process began by combining L-cystine (0.065 g) and o-phenylenediamine (0.2 g) in 10 mL of ethanol to create the initial reaction mixture. This solution underwent thermal treatment in a specialized hydrothermal reactor equipped with a Teflon liner, where it was maintained at a precise temperature of 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction period, the mixture was cooled to room temperature naturally before proceeding with a multi-step purification protocol. The first purification step involved centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min to separate and remove larger particles. The obtained supernatant was then subjected to filtration using a 0.22 μm membrane, followed by an extensive dialysis step. The dialysis process, utilizing a membrane with a 1000 Da molecular weight cutoff, was conducted for 48 h to ensure the complete removal of unreacted starting materials and byproducts. To obtain the final product, the purified carbon dot solution underwent lyophilization, yielding a stable powder form. The synthesized D@CDs were stored at 4 °C under refrigeration to preserve their properties for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield of the prepared D@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The calculations for quantum yield are included in the Supplementary Data file.

The detection system was prepared by dissolving D@CDs (1.0 mg/mL, 500 µL) in a clean reaction vessel. Cu2+ solution (100.0 µg/mL, 1000 µL) was added, followed by varying concentrations of the target analyte. Britton-Robinson buffer (pH 8.0) was used to adjust the final volume. Fluorescence measurements were conducted under optimized conditions, with excitation at 270 nm and dual-emission monitoring at 370 nm and 578 nm. Both excitation and emission monochromators were set to a 5 nm bandpass to ensure optimal sensitivity and reproducibility.

The biological sample preparation protocol was adopted from previously reported work with some modifications [19]. For serum samples, 250 μL was diluted with 800 μL of the same ammonium bicarbonate solution. The diluted samples then underwent solid-phase extraction for purification and concentration. The SPE cartridge (Oasis HLB cartridge) was first prepared by sequential conditioning with 3 mL methanol followed by 3 mL water to activate the sorbent material. A 700 μL aliquot of the diluted sample was then carefully loaded onto the conditioned cartridge. To remove interfering compounds while retaining ZOL, the cartridge underwent a two-step washing procedure using 2 mL water followed by 1.5 mL methanol. The solution was then eluted from the cartridge using methanol (500 μL). The collected eluate was concentrated by evaporation under a gentle stream of nitrogen at 40 °C until completely dry. Finally, the purified sample was reconstituted in 500 μL of a methanol–water mixture (1:9 v/v) for subsequent fluorometric analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Induction of chronic renal impairment in experimental animals
ce:section.ce:para: The study used eighteen Sprague-Dawley rats (both sexes), aged 8–10 weeks and weighing 200–250 g. They were housed individually in metabolic cages under controlled conditions (25 ± 2 °C, 50–60 % humidity, 12-hour light–dark cycle). The rats had free access to standard rodent feed and water. A seven-day acclimatization period was conducted to monitor their health, feeding, and behavior before the experiment. Chronic renal failure was induced using cisplatin, administered intravenously at 6 mg/kg/day for seven consecutive days. The drug, prepared in sterile saline, was delivered via the tail vein under aseptic conditions. Renal impairment was confirmed by assessing serum creatinine and urinary protein levels. The protocol was adapted from validated studies [20,21], with dose adjustments based on body surface area calculations to ensure effectiveness and safety in rats.

Blood was collected from Sprague-Dawley rats' lateral tail veins (1 mL per sample) at specific intervals (0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 5, 7, 9, 12, 18, and 24 h) after administering ZOL through intravenous infusion. These samples were gathered in tubes with clot activator and left to coagulate at ambient temperature for 30 min. Following coagulation, the samples underwent centrifugation (4000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C) to extract the serum. The resulting serum was promptly transferred to clean microcentrifuge tubes and preserved at −80 °C until needed for analysis. A three-compartment model in pK solver software was used to determine the pharmacokinetic parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological studies of D@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of the synthesized carbon dots revealed detailed morphological characteristics and size distribution (Fig. 1 A). The micrographs demonstrated that the particles possess a uniform, near-spherical morphology with excellent dispersion, indicating successful control over the synthesis process and minimal aggregation. Quantitative analysis through size distribution histograms showed that the carbon dots exhibit a relatively narrow size range between 2.2 and 6.9 nm, with a mean diameter of 4.6 nm, confirming their quantum-sized nature. This size range is particularly significant as it falls within the optimal range for quantum confinement effects, which directly influences their photoluminescent properties, making these carbon dots suitable for fluorescence-based sensing applications. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the synthesized carbon dots exhibits a characteristic broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 26.15°, which corresponds to the (002) lattice plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1B). The broad and diffuse nature of this peak, rather than a sharp crystalline reflection, indicates that the carbon dots possess a partially graphitized structure with short-range ordered domains, typical of amorphous carbon materials that contain both sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a disordered manner within their core structure [22]. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic analysis of the carbon dots revealed several characteristic absorption bands that provide insight into their surface functional groups and molecular structure (Fig. 1C). The broad, intense peak at 3366 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups and primary/secondary amines on the carbon dot surface [23]. The peak at 2896 cm−1 can be linked to the CH stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of aliphatic hydrocarbon groups. The absorption band at 1535 cm−1 represents CC stretching vibrations and/or NH bending vibrations, confirming the presence of aromatic structures and amino groups. The peak at 1315 cm−1 can be attributed to CN stretching vibrations, further supporting the successful incorporation of nitrogen-containing functional groups, while the band at 971 cm−1 corresponds to COC stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of ether linkages in the carbon dot structure. This spectral profile confirms the successful functionalization of the carbon dots with various oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups, which contribute to their unique optical and chemical properties [24]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the synthesized carbon dots revealed a comprehensive surface composition profile through distinct binding energy peaks. The survey spectrum exhibited four primary elements: carbon (C 1s) at 284.77 eV, nitrogen (N 1s) at 402.42 eV, oxygen (O 1s) at 533.74 eV, and sulfur (S 2p) at 165.52 eV, confirming the successful incorporation of all intended elements during synthesis (Fig. 1D). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three characteristic peaks at 284.71, 286.04, and 288.14 eV, corresponding to CC/CC (sp2/sp3 carbon), CN/CO (carbon-heteroatom single bonds), and CO/COOH (carbonyl/carboxyl groups) respectively (Fig. S1A) [25]. The N 1s spectrum showed three distinct components at 398.30, 399.52, and 400.41 eV, which can be attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, indicating diverse nitrogen configurations within the carbon dot structure (Fig. S1B). The O 1s spectrum revealed three peaks at 529.71, 531.13, and 532.14 eV, assigned to CO (carbonyl), CO (hydroxyl), and OH/COOH (carboxyl) groups (Fig. S1C) [26], while the S 2p spectrum exhibited two characteristic peaks at 162.23 and 163.25 eV, corresponding to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 spin–orbit coupling of thiophenic sulfur (CSC) (Fig. S1D) [27], demonstrating the successful integration of sulfur into the carbon dot framework through covalent bonding.

The optical properties of the synthesized D@CDs were thoroughly analyzed using spectroscopic techniques, offering valuable information about their electronic structure and highlighting their potential for use in sensing applications. The UV–visible absorption spectrum exhibited four characteristic peaks: an absorption at 232 nm attributed to π–π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds, a peak at 272 nm corresponding to n–π* transitions of CO bonds, and two additional peaks at 340 nm and 421 nm indicating the presence of multiple chromophoric groups and surface states within the carbon dot structure (Fig. 2 A) [28]. Most notably, the fluorescence spectroscopy unveiled a remarkable dual-emission behavior with peaks at 370 nm and 578 nm when excited at 270 nm, suggesting the presence of two distinct emissive states within the carbon dot framework [29]. The excitation-independent nature of these emission wavelengths, observed across an excitation range of 230–290 nm (Fig. 2B), indicates that the emission originates from specific, well-defined energy states rather than the heterogeneous surface states typically associated with carbon dots [29]. The optimal excitation wavelength was identified at 270 nm, which yielded maximum emission intensity for both peaks, aligning well with the absorption peak at 272 nm and suggesting efficient energy transfer to both emissive states. The quantum yield measurement of 19.96 % demonstrates relatively high fluorescence efficiency.

The stability of the synthesized D@CDs was comprehensively studied under various environmental conditions by monitoring the ratiometric fluorescence signal (F578/F370), which serves as a sensitive indicator of the system's response to external factors. The pH dependency study across a range of 1–11 revealed an inverse relationship between pH and the fluorescence intensity ratio, where increasing pH values led to a decrease in the ratio due to the selective quenching of the 578 nm emission peak (Fig. S2A). This behavior suggests that the electronic states responsible for the longer wavelength emission are more sensitive to proton concentration, possibly due to protonation/deprotonation effects on surface functional groups [30]. When examining ionic strength stability using NaCl concentrations from 0.05 to 1.0 M (Fig. S2B), the carbon dots demonstrated remarkable resistance to ionic interference, maintaining consistent ratiometric signals across the entire range – a highly desirable characteristic for sensing applications in complex biological matrices. The photostability investigation, conducted through UV exposure for up to 60 min (Fig. S2C), showed only a slight increase in the fluorescence ratio, indicating good resistance to photobleaching with minimal alteration of the dual-emission properties. Temperature stability studies from 20 to 80 °C revealed that the carbon dots maintain their ratiometric fluorescence properties consistently up to 60 °C (Fig. S2D), beyond which changes in the ratio become apparent. This comprehensive stability profile demonstrates the robustness of these dual-emission carbon dots under various conditions, making them promising candidates for reliable sensing applications.

The optimization process for ZOL detection using the D@CDs/Cu2+ sensing system involved careful investigation of three critical parameters that directly influence the system's analytical performance. The first crucial parameter explored was the concentration of copper ions, which was systematically varied from 0 to 300 μg/mL to determine its effect on the dual-emission behavior of the carbon dots (Fig. S3A). The addition of Cu2+ ions induced a distinct ratiometric response, characterized by an enhancement of the 370 nm emission peak accompanied by a simultaneous decrease in the 578 nm emission, likely due to the selective interaction of copper ions with surface functional groups responsible for the longer wavelength emission. The optimal copper concentration was identified at 100 μg/mL, where the lowest F578/F370 ratio was achieved, establishing the most favorable baseline for subsequent ZOL detection. The pH dependence of the detection system was then evaluated across a range of 3–11 (Fig. S3B), with pH 8 emerging as the optimal condition yielding the highest F578/F370 ratio upon ZOL addition. This pH optimization is particularly significant as it reflects the condition where ZOL most effectively complexes with Cu2+ ions, leading to maximal restoration of the carbon dots' original fluorescence properties. The time of the detection process was also optimized by monitoring the system's response over a 15-minute period (Fig. S3C), revealing that the maximum fluorescence restoration ratio was achieved within just 2 min of ZOL addition. This rapid response time, combined with the optimized copper ions concentration and pH conditions, demonstrates the practical utility of this sensing system for efficient ZOL detection.

The dual-emission fluorescent probe exhibited a distinctive visual color transition from bright violet to orange when illuminated under a 254 nm UV lamp as ZOL concentrations increased from 10 nM to 90 nM (Fig. 3C). This visible color change enables detection of ZOL without specialized equipment. Fig. 3D presents the Commission Internationale de l'éclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates corresponding to the fluorescence emissions shown in Fig. 3C [31]. These coordinates demonstrate a clear progression across the chromaticity diagram as ZOL concentration increases, moving systematically from the violet region through light purple and finally to the orange area. This visual transformation confirms the method's capacity for both quantitative analysis and qualitative visual assessment of ZOL. The combination of observable color changes and their corresponding positions on the chromaticity diagram provides an intuitive and accessible approach to ZOL detection that offers significant practical advantages for analytical applications.

The dual-emission sensing system (D@CDs/Cu2+) was extensively evaluated for its ability to selectively detect ZOL in the presence of numerous potentially interfering substances commonly encountered in biological samples. The comprehensive selectivity study examined a wide array of compounds that could potentially affect the sensing performance, including amino acids (histidine, cysteine), organic acids (citric acid, lactic acid, uric acid), therapeutic agents commonly prescribed alongside ZOL (vitamin D3, teriparatide), common medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, aspirin), biological molecules (creatinine, BSA, glucose), chemotherapy drugs (cisplatin, paclitaxel), and physiologically relevant ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, Cl−, HCO3 −, NO3 −) (Fig. S4). Under optimized experimental conditions, each potential interferent was systematically evaluated by comparing its effect on the ratiometric fluorescence signal (F578/F370) against that of ZOL. The sensing platform exhibited exceptional molecular recognition capabilities, with a distinctive fluorescence response observed exclusively in the presence of ZOL, while other tested substances showed minimal interference with the analytical signal. This remarkable selectivity validates the method's reliability for accurate ZOL quantification in complex biological matrices where multiple potential interferents coexist.

The detection mechanism of ZOL using the D@CDs/Cu2+ system relies on a sophisticated interplay of molecular interactions that produce distinct spectroscopic signatures. The initial interaction between copper ions and the dual-emission carbon dots triggers a remarkable dual-signal response: an enhancement of the shorter wavelength emission at 370 nm coupled with simultaneous quenching of the longer wavelength emission at 578 nm. This unique response pattern likely stems from Cu2+ ions selectively interacting with specific surface functional groups on the carbon dots, modifying their electronic states and energy transfer pathways. When ZOL is introduced to this system, its strong chelating properties enable it to effectively complex with the copper ions, essentially removing them from their interaction sites on the carbon dots. This complexation process induces a reversal of the initial spectroscopic changes, restoring both emission peaks towards their original intensities in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 4 A and Fig. 3A). To elucidate the underlying mechanisms of these interactions, a comprehensive spectroscopic investigation was undertaken using multiple complementary techniques.

The mechanistic investigation of the D@CDs/Cu2+ system began with detailed spectroscopic analysis to understand the fundamental interactions governing the sensing process. The absence of spectral overlap between ZOL's absorption spectrum and either the excitation or emission spectra of the carbon dots confirms that ZOL does not directly influence the carbon dots' photophysical properties (Fig. 4B). Upon introduction of Cu2+ ions to the carbon dots, significant changes were observed in the UV–visible absorption spectrum, characterized by bathochromic shifts and intensity enhancements of the peaks at 340 nm and 421 nm (Fig. 4B). These spectral alterations strongly suggest the formation of a ground-state complex between Cu2+ ions and the surface functional groups of the carbon dots, likely involving interactions with amino, thiol, and aromatic moieties inherited from the precursor molecules. The formation of this ground-state complex leads to a unique dual response in the fluorescence behavior: quenching of the longer wavelength emission at 578 nm while simultaneously enhancing the shorter wavelength emission at 370 nm. This distinctive response pattern suggests that the Cu2+ complexation selectively modifies different electronic states within the carbon dot structure, possibly through differential interactions with various surface functional groups. The addition of ZOL to the D@CDs/Cu2+ system results in a decrease in intensity for both the 340 nm and 421 nm peaks, accompanied by a blue shift. This spectral change provides strong evidence of complex formation between ZOL and Cu2+, indicating their effective interaction (Fig. 4C). Upon the introduction of Cu2+, the zeta potential of D@CDs (Fig. 4D) shifts from −6.85 mV to +17.7 mV, likely due to the removal of surface anions, indicating successful interaction between Cu2+ and D@CDs. Following the addition of ZOL, the zeta potential decreases to +1.12 mV, suggesting further modifications in surface charge distribution, likely due to the complexation between ZOL and Cu2+.

ZOL has a strong affinity for copper ions due to its chemical structure as a bisphosphonate containing multiple binding sites [35]. The molecule features two phosphonate groups and a nitrogen-containing heterocyclic ring, which can act as electron donors. The phosphonate groups are particularly important, as they can form stable chelate complexes with copper ions through their oxygen atoms [36]. The spatial arrangement of these binding sites creates an optimal geometry for coordinating with copper ions, allowing multiple points of attachment [37]. This multidentate binding capability, combined with the hard base character of the phosphonate oxygens matching well with the hard acid character of Cu2+, results in the formation of thermodynamically stable metal complexes. The presence of the imidazole ring provides additional coordination possibilities, further enhancing the overall binding affinity [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of ZOL in spiked rat serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The research validated a novel method for detecting ZOL in rat serum specimens. The experimental approach successfully measured ZOL concentrations in rat serum across a range of 10–90.0 nM. The proposed method is capable of accurately determining real sample concentrations, ensuring reliable quantification of clinically relevant levels, which have been reported to range between 100 and 3700 nM depending on the administered dose and patient condition [8]. Analysis revealed a linear relationship between fluorescence ratios (F578/F370) and concentration, yielding the equation F578/F370 = 0.0068C + 0.27, with strong correlation (R2 = 0.9938) (Fig. S5). The method demonstrated high sensitivity with a detection limit of 4.02 nM. To verify practical applicability, the technique was used to quantify ZOL in actual rat serum samples. The results showed impressive accuracy with recovery rates of 97.30–100.50 % and good precision indicated by relative standard deviations under 2.98 % (Table 2 ). The method's validity was confirmed through comparative analysis with HPLC-ESI-MS [12], showing consistent recovery percentages between both approaches. This agreement with established HPLC methodology substantiates the reliability of this new analytical technique for measuring ZOL in biological matrices.

The research team applied their spectrofluorometric technique to measure ZOL concentrations in serum from both healthy rats and those with induced kidney impairment, enabling pharmacokinetic evaluation. The method's calibration ranges proved suitable for analyzing serum samples following a dose of ZOL. The observed serum concentrations throughout the pharmacokinetic study remained within the validated calibration limits for both experimental groups. The drug's pharmacokinetic behavior in healthy and kidney-impaired rats was depicted graphically in Fig. 5 , with the corresponding pharmacokinetic parameters compiled in Table S1.

The pharmacokinetic analysis of ZOL in healthy and renal-impaired (diseased) rats highlights significant alterations in drug disposition. In renal dysfunction, the elimination rate constant (k10) decreases (0.1796 h−1–0.0637 h−1), indicating impaired clearance (CL: 0.00185 mg/ng/ml/h in healthy vs. 0.000612 mg/ng/ml/h in diseased). The half-life of the beta (t1/2β) and gamma (t1/2γ) phases are prolonged (2.90 h–8.97 h and 6.86 h–19.66 h, respectively), confirming delayed drug elimination and extended systemic exposure. The steady-state concentration (Css) in diseased rats is significantly higher (27,012 ng/mL vs. 81,633 ng/mL), reflecting excessive drug accumulation. Similarly, the area under the curve (AUC0-∞) is nearly threefold higher (2,160.99 ng/mL.h vs. 6,530.67 ng/mL.h), emphasizing prolonged drug retention. These findings indicate that standard dosing in renal impairment results in excessive drug accumulation, increasing the risk of toxicity, including nephrotoxicity and hypocalcemia. Dose recommendations for patients with renal dysfunction include reducing the dose, extending dosing intervals, and frequent renal function monitoring (creatinine clearance, serum creatinine). A lower initial dose with gradual titration based on renal function markers is necessary to prevent toxicity while maintaining therapeutic efficacy.

In conclusion, this research introduces the first ratiometric fluorometric sensor for zoledronic acid detection based on a D@CDs/Cu2+ system, representing a significant advancement in analytical methodology for this important bisphosphonate. The sensor operates through a well-characterized mechanism whereby zoledronic acid forms stable complexes with copper ions, inducing measurable changes in dual fluorescence emissions that enable highly sensitive detection. Our comprehensive investigation established excellent analytical performance characteristics, including an impressive limit of detection, wide linear range, and exceptional correlation coefficients, making this method suitable for clinical applications. The practical utility of the developed sensor was successfully demonstrated through its application to real biological samples, where it accurately quantified zoledronic acid levels in rat serum with excellent recovery rates comparable to standard instrumental techniques. Furthermore, the method's application to pharmacokinetic studies in both healthy and renally impaired rats provided valuable insights into how renal dysfunction affects zoledronic acid disposition, information that could significantly impact clinical dosing strategies. This novel analytical approach offers a simpler, more accessible alternative to traditional methods while maintaining high sensitivity and selectivity, potentially enhancing therapeutic drug monitoring capabilities and contributing to improved patient outcomes in clinical settings where zoledronic acid is administered.

Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Supervision, Software, Resources, Data curation, Conceptualization. Aya M. Mostafa: Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology. James Barker: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Resources. Yousef A. Bin Jardan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113704.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
